docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Alvoc

Abundance learning for variants of concern

Report Bug | Request Feature

Documentation

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Alvoc is a tool for estimating the abundace of variants of concern from wastewater sequencing data. Its predecessor is Alcov, an abundance learning tool for SARS-CoV-2 variants.

In addition alvoc can also be used for:

  • Determining the frequency of mutations of interest in BAM files
  • Converting nucleotide and amino acid mutations for a particular virus
  • Comparing amplicon GC content with its read depth (as a measure of degredation)

docs.title: With Pip
docs.text:
Pip is the default package installer for Python.

  1. Install Python. We recommend using pyenv for python version management.

  2. Install the latest version from pypi.

pip install alvoc

docs.title: With UV
docs.text:
UV is a fast, all-in-one Python tool for dependency, version, and project management, replacing pip, poetry, and more.

  1. Install UV.
  2. Install the latest version from pypi.
uv add alvoc

Yes, including a Docker image under the installation section makes sense, especially for users who prefer containerized environments or need to integrate your tool into CI/CD pipelines. Here's how you can include it:

docs.title: With Docker
docs.text:
For users who prefer containerized environments, an official Docker image is available on GitHub Container Registry (GHCR).

  1. Ensure you have Docker installed and running.
  2. Pull the latest Docker image:
docker pull ghcr.io/alvoc/alvoc:latest
  1. Run the container:
docker run --rm -it ghcr.io/alvoc/alvoc:latest

docs.title: What is Alvoc?
docs.text: Alvoc is a command-line tool designed to analyze viral sequencing data with a focus on identifying variants of concern and their defining mutations. With the ongoing rise of infectious diseases like SARS-CoV-2, monitoring and understanding viral evolution has become critical for researchers, public health officials, and laboratories.

docs.title: Why Use Alvoc?
docs.text:
1. Lineage Identification

    • Alvoc helps determine which viral lineages are present in your sequencing data. For example, it can identify emerging lineages like B.1.1.7 (Alpha) or P.1 (Gamma) for SARS-CoV-2.
    • Use Alvoc when you need to:
      • Compare samples to known lineage-defining mutations.
      • Detect specific lineages in samples from an outbreak.
      • Include or exclude specific lineages (whitelisting/blacklisting).

  2. Mutation Detection

    • Alvoc allows you to detect mutations in sequencing data with customizable depth thresholds.
    • Use this feature to:
      • Find known or novel mutations in viral samples.
      • Focus on specific mutations like S:N501Y or E484K that are critical for vaccine efficacy or transmissibility.

  3. Amplicon Quality Assessment

    • Viral sequencing often relies on amplicon-based approaches. Alvoc provides tools to assess amplicon metrics like coverage and GC content.
    • This helps ensure high-quality sequencing data for downstream analyses.

docs.title: When to Use Alvoc?
docs.text:
You should use Alvoc if you are:

  • A researcher analyzing viral sequencing data to study genetic variation and evolution.
  • A public health analyst monitoring variants of concern during outbreaks.
  • A bioinformatician processing large-scale sequencing data to identify mutations and lineages.
  • A laboratory scientist validating amplicon-based sequencing data for accuracy.

Alvoc simplifies these workflows into a set of streamlined commands, saving time and improving consistency across analyses.

docs.title: alvoc
docs.text:
Abundance learning for variants of concern

Usage:

alvoc [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Options:

Name Type Description Default --version boolean Current version of Alvoc None --install-completion boolean Install completion for the current shell. None --show-completion boolean Show completion for the current shell, to copy it or customize the installation. None --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

Subcommands

  • amplicons: Get amplicon metrics such as coverage, gc_content and visualizations
  • convert: Tools to convert mutations
  • extract-gene-data: Extracts gene coordinates and the genome sequence from a GenBank file or generates them using an Entrez API search term.
  • find-lineages: Find lineages in samples
  • find-mutants: Find mutations in sequencing data, either from BAM files or a sample list.
  • make-constellations: Generates constellations using the provided nextstrain phylogeny dataset.

docs.title: amplicons
docs.text:
Get amplicon metrics such as coverage, gc_content and visualizations

Usage:

alvoc amplicons [OPTIONS] VIRUS SAMPLES INSERTS_PATH

Options:

Name Type Description Default --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: convert
docs.text:
Tools to convert mutations

Usage:

alvoc convert [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Options:

Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

Subcommands

  • aa: Convert amino acid mutation to nucleotide mutations for a given virus.
  • nt: Convert nucleotide mutation to amino acid mutation for a given virus.

docs.title: aa
docs.text:
Convert amino acid mutation to nucleotide mutations for a given virus.

Usage:

alvoc convert aa [OPTIONS] VIRUS MUT

Options:

Name Type Description Default --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: nt
docs.text:
Convert nucleotide mutation to amino acid mutation for a given virus.

Usage:

alvoc convert nt [OPTIONS] VIRUS MUT

Options:

Name Type Description Default --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: extract-gene-data
docs.text:
Extracts gene coordinates and the genome sequence from a GenBank file or generates them using an Entrez API search term.

Usage:

alvoc extract-gene-data [OPTIONS] VIRUS

Options:

Name Type Description Default --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: find-lineages
docs.text:
Find lineages in samples

Usage:

alvoc find-lineages [OPTIONS] VIRUS SAMPLES CONSTELLATIONS

Options:

Name Type Description Default --white-list, -wl path Path to a TXT file containing lineages to inclue. None --black-list, -bl path Path to a TXT file containing lineages to exclude. None --min-depth, -d integer Minimum depth for a mutation to be considered. 40 --unique, -u boolean Whether to consider unique mutations only. False --l2, -l2 boolean Whether to use a secondary method for regression analysis. False --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: find-mutants
docs.text:
Find mutations in sequencing data, either from BAM files or a sample list.

Usage:

alvoc find-mutants [OPTIONS] VIRUS SAMPLES CONSTELLATIONS MUTATIONS

Options:

Name Type Description Default --min-depth, -d integer Minimum depth 10 --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: make-constellations
docs.text:
Generates constellations using the provided nextstrain phylogeny dataset.

Usage:

alvoc make-constellations [OPTIONS] TREE_URL

Options:

Name Type Description Default --proportion_threshold, -pt integer Minimum proportion of nodes in a clade required to include a mutation 1 --outdir, -o text Output directory for results and intermediate data . --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
This guide explains how to use Alvoc, highlighting key features, input formats, and examples. It assumes you've already installed the tool. If not, please the installation instructions.

With Alvoc, you can quickly move from raw sequencing data to actionable insights about viral mutations and lineages.

docs.title: Pre-requisites
docs.text:
Alvoc, like many other downstream analysis tools, requires you to have some upstream data to unlock its functionality. There are 2 main requirements for the core commands:

  1. A GB (genbank) file for a complete genome of the virus to analyze, or an Entrez api search term. Please see the Extracting Gene Data section for a brief on how this works.

  2. A sorted BAM file, or a CSV samplesheet listing samples and absolute paths to the BAM files. An example csv file:

sample bam sample1 /absolute/path/to/sample1.bam sample2 /absolute/path/to/sample2.bam

docs.title: First steps
docs.text:
Alvoc comes with a variety of tools to help you with abundance learning. Take a gander by running the help flag to see a general help message:

alvoc --helpUsage: alvoc [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...Abundance learning for variants of concernOptions: --version Show current version. --install-completion Install shell completion. --show-completion Show shell completion code. --help Show this message and exit.Commands: find-lineages Find lineages in samples. find-mutants Identify mutations in sequencing data. amplicons Get amplicon metrics like coverage and GC content. extract-gene-data Extract gene data from GenBank or Entrez. convert Convert amino acid and nucleotide mutations.

docs.title: Extracting Gene Data
docs.text: Before we take a look at some of the core commands, it is important to understand some of Alvoc's internals. Each command requires gene coordinates and sequence data. We can extract this from genbank files, or we can auto-fetch the data for you using an Entrez search term.

docs.title: A brief on the Entrez API
docs.text:
The Entrez API (E-utilities) is a tool provided by the NCBI to interact with its biological databases, such as GenBank and PubMed. It allows you to search for IDs, retrieve data like sequences, and link related records across databases. We use this internally to download a genbank file programatically.

You can test how it works using the following examples to get a feel for it:

  1. Access by accession id:
alvoc extract-gene-data NC_045512.2
  1. You can also just search by taxid, although you need to pass it like this:
alvoc extract-gene-data "txid2697049[Organism:exp]"
  1. Sometimes you want to be even more specific
alvoc extract-gene-data "txid2697049[Organism:exp] AND \"Wuhan-Hu-1\" AND complete genome"

Note

Every command in alvoc requires this input or alternatively a genbank file. In general, we recommend using the accession ID for data retrieval, as it is the most straightforward and unambiguous approach. However, the ability to search by taxonomy ID and refine queries provides flexibility for more complex use cases.

docs.title: Finding Lineages
docs.text: The find-lineages command identifies viral lineages from sequencing data. Running it will output a lineage abundance heatmap and two csv files.

docs.title: Command Example
docs.text: alvoc find-lineages some_accession_id samples.csv constellations.json --outdir ./results

docs.title: Constellations
docs.text:
A key requirement for the find-lineages command is is a json input file containing lineage-centric data. We use the term "constellations" to convey how lineage-defining mutations are organized. Constellations should include a list of site mutations in nucleotide format.

For ease of use, we provide a make-constellations command for generation this file using nexstrain trees.

alvoc make_constellations <nexstrain_tree_dataset_url> --outdir .

While the above command is useful, nexstrain has a limited set of pathogens, and in addition, it may not capture all the lineage data necessary for your experiment. For those cases, we recommend generating your own constellations file. An example of how this data should look like is below:

   {
     "A.23.1": {
        "lineage": "A.23.1",
        "label": "A.23.1-like",
        "description": "A.23.1 lineage defining mutations",
        "sources": [],
        "tags": [
            "A.23.1"
        ],
        "sites": [
            "C4573T",
            "C8782T",
            "C10747T",
            "G11230T",
            "G11266T",
            "G11521T",
            "C16575T",
            "C17745T",
            "C22000T",
            "C22033A",
            "G22661T",
            "G23401T",
            "C23604G",
            "T24097C",
            "T28144C",
            "G28167A",
            "G28378C",
            "G28878A",
            "G29742A"
        ],
        "note": "Unique mutations for sublineage"
    },
    ...
   }

We provide some example scripts for Sars-CoV-2 Pango lineage constellations.

docs.title: Finding Mutations
docs.text: The find-mutants command identifies specific mutations from sequencing and outputs a csv file containing occurences and non-occurences of each mutation, alongside a heatmap.

docs.title: Command Example
docs.text:
alvoc find-mutants virus.gb samples.csv constellations.json mutations.txt --outdir ./results

Here's what the mutations.txt file might look like

S:N501Y
G23012A

docs.title: Amplicon Metrics
docs.text:
The amplicons command analyzes amplicon metrics like coverage and GC content. You'll need to provide:

A BED file, containing your inserts of interest. For example:

MN908947.3  50  408 SARS-CoV-2_INSERT_1 1   +
MN908947.3  344 705 SARS-CoV-2_INSERT_2 2   +
MN908947.3  666 1017    SARS-CoV-2_INSERT_3 1   +

docs.title: Command Example
docs.text: alvoc amplicons virus.gb samples.csv inserts.bed --outdir ./results

docs.title: Converting Mutations
docs.text: The convert command transforms mutations between formats.

docs.title: Amino Acid to Nucleotide
docs.text: alvoc convert aa virus S:N501Y

docs.title: Nucleotide to Amino Acid
docs.text: alvoc convert nt virus G23012A
